Physical Therapist
PT, DPT Guide

Issued by the Minnesota Board of Medical Practice — PT under the Minnesota Department of Health. Data verified for 2026.

Visit Official Board Portal
Fees Board Contact License Types How to Apply Documents Reciprocity FAQ
Total Initial Fee
$100
$100 application/license fee
Renewal
$50
Every 2 years
Processing Time
4 to 6 Weeks
Subject to board volume
License Duration
2 Years
From date of issuance

Credential Categories

Minnesota Physical Therapist License Types

PT, DPT

The standard credential to practice as a physical therapist in Minnesota.

Application Process

How to Apply for a Minnesota PT, DPT

Complete Education Requirements

Complete a CAPTE-accredited Doctor of Physical Therapy (DPT) program and any required clinical practicum or fieldwork.

Pass the NPTE

Successfully pass the required NPTE (National Physical Therapy Examination).

Submit Application and Fees

Complete the state application, undergo a background check if required, and submit the necessary licensing fees.

Required Documents

Application Document Checklist

  • Official TranscriptsSent directly from your educational institution.
  • Exam Score ReportOfficial verification of passing the NPTE (National Physical Therapy Examination).
Practice Laws

Provisional Practice & Reciprocity

Provisional Rules

Many states offer a limited permit or provisional license for new graduates waiting to take the NPTE. Check specific board rules for supervisory requirements.

Reciprocity & Endorsement

Physical Therapists licensed in another state can often apply via endorsement by providing license verification and current certification.

Frequently Asked Questions

Minnesota Physical Therapist FAQ

How much does a Minnesota physical therapist license cost?

The initial application and licensing fee varies, often around $100 to $200. Check the specific fee breakdown above.

Is national certification required?

Yes, passing the NPTE (National Physical Therapy Examination) is a standard requirement for initial licensure across almost all states.
